laws.title: Public Law 16-122 | The Congressional Competency Act of 2023
laws.chronology_conclusion: No recoverable operative chronology can be fixed because the source text is unavailable. The act should stay source-limited until the authentic enactment is recovered.
laws.target_justification: No validated operative text was recoverable, so there is no safe codification target.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep only a source-limited historical note; do not assert repeal, supersession, or a permanent Code placement.
source_evidence: Primary report marks the source unavailable.
The available source is a permission-denial page, not operative text.
Source text is not recoverable.
Primary report treats the law as source-unavailable.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: No corrected codification was confirmed on the reviewed branch or in origin/main.
current_xml_comparison.detail: The current XML contains only a short note at Title 2 section 1 and adds a repeal or supersession label that the source cannot support.

final_recommended_action: Retain only a historical note.
Remove the unsupported repeal or supersession label.
Re-acquire the authentic source before any substantive codification.

laws.title: Public Law 17-124 | Law Enforcement Accountability Act (LEAA)
laws.chronology_conclusion: Operative general law-enforcement enactment; no later repeal was identified in the reviewed materials.
laws.target_justification: The source creates standalone law-enforcement standards and does not map cleanly to a single existing Code section.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the short title as note material only.
source_evidence: The act cites the Law Enforcement Accountability Act or LEAA.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the severability and anti-supersession language as savings note content.
source_evidence: The act says it shall not be superseded unless a later law says so.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the immediate-effective-date clause as a short note.
source_evidence: The act becomes effective upon passage and signature.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a new permanent definitions section for apprehension, bite, canine handler, force levels, and related terms.
source_evidence: Section 4 is a definitions block.
It defines apprehension, bite, canine handler, and objective reasonableness.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a new permanent canine-operations section covering tactical and non-tactical deployment, call-off duties, and handler responsibilities.
source_evidence: The act authorizes canine deployments for tactical and non-tactical assignments.
It requires call-off, medical care, and training rules.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a new permanent use-of-force section with objective reasonableness, de-escalation, proportionality, and deadly-force limits.
source_evidence: The text says law enforcement shall only use force that is objectively reasonable.
It also requires de-escalation whenever feasible.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a new enforcement section or a tightly trimmed note attached to the new law-enforcement material.
source_evidence: The Attorney General and other agency leadership shall enact policies and procedures.
The source is readable and operative.
It creates definitions, canine rules, use-of-force rules, and enforcement duties.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: The reviewed branch keeps only a note; origin/main did not reveal the required operative sections either.
current_xml_comparison.detail: The current XML is just a short note at Title 18 section 1 and does not carry the substantive canine or use-of-force rules as real Code text.

final_recommended_action: Codify the substantive rules as real permanent sections.
Keep only the short-title, severability, and effective-date material as note content.
Remove the Trello link from the current note.

laws.title: Public Law 17-125 | We Don't Read Minds Act of 2023
laws.chronology_conclusion: Live reporting-duty statute; no repeal was identified in the reviewed materials.
laws.target_justification: The act imposes a reporting duty and penalty mechanism, but the reviewed sources did not identify a precise existing section to amend.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the short title as note material only.
source_evidence: The act may be cited as the We Don't Read Minds Act.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the purposes language as explanatory note content.
source_evidence: The purpose is to mandate monthly updates to Congress.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a permanent Title 5 reporting-duty section requiring each executive department Inspector General to submit monthly reports to Congress.
source_evidence: The Inspector General from each executive department must submit monthly reports.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a penalty and enforcement subsection for failure to report, including the House inquiry mechanism and the $10,000 fine language.
source_evidence: The OIG may be fined up to $10,000.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the immediate-effective-date clause as a short note.
source_evidence: The bill takes effect immediately after passage and presidential signature.
The act requires monthly reports from each executive department IG.
It includes a penalty for non-reporting.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: The reviewed branch still shows the same note-only problem; origin/main did not show a corrected live reporting statute.
current_xml_comparison.detail: The current XML note is anchored in Title 5 section 101 and incorrectly labels the law repealed or superseded.

final_recommended_action: Keep the act as a live Title 5 reporting statute.
Replace the repeal label with current-status treatment.
Remove the Trello link.

laws.title: Public Law 17-126 | Special Events Act
laws.chronology_conclusion: The special-events framework is later superseded by PL 031-226, so it should remain historical only and not be carried forward as permanent Code text.
laws.target_justification: The source is a District of Columbia special-events enactment that was later superseded, so there is no permanent federal Code target.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the short title as a historical note only.
source_evidence: The act is called the Special Events Act or SEA.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the severability and anti-supersession language only as historical context.
source_evidence: The act says it shall not be superseded unless a later law expressly says so.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Do not move the local permit rules into permanent Code text; keep only a concise local-history note if one is retained.
source_evidence: The permits are for special events within the District of Columbia.
The enforcement rules are local and administrative.
The source is a special-events permitting law for the District of Columbia.
A later enactment supersedes it.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: The reviewed branch still places the law in Title 50; origin/main did not show a corrected local-history placement.
current_xml_comparison.detail: The current XML note is parked in Title 50 and labels the law repealed or superseded, but the source is a District of Columbia special-events statute.

final_recommended_action: Do not treat this as Title 50 material.
Keep only a concise local-history note if one is retained.
Remove the unsupported repeal label.

laws.title: Public Law 17-127 | Border Security Act
laws.chronology_conclusion: Operative repeal-and-rewrite enactment. Later sequencing must account for the later repeal of 8 U.S.C. 1102 by PL 19-147, but the PL 17-127 rewrite chain itself remains current.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the Public Law 1-1 applicability statement as a short explanatory note.
source_evidence: The act invokes Public Law 1-1 for section applicability conventions.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Mark Title 8 as repealed in sequence, then carry forward the later reenactments and rewrites in this act.
source_evidence: U.S. Code: Title 8 is repealed in its entirety.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Replace the named sections with the supplied 1101, 1551, 1102, 1103, and 1105 text.
source_evidence: 8 U.S. Code 1101 shall now read as.
8 U.S. Code 1551 shall now read as.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Replace 8 U.S. Code 1182 and 1189 with the supplied inadmissibility and designation text.
source_evidence: 8 U.S. Code 1182 shall read as.
8 U.S. Code 1189 shall now read as.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Replace sections 1532, 1533, 1534, and 1481 with the supplied removal and nationality text.
source_evidence: 8 U.S. Code 1532 shall now read as.
8 U.S. Code 1481 shall now read as.
The act repeals Title 8 and rewrites multiple listed sections.
It contains direct replacement text for the immigration and removal chain.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: The reviewed branch only exposes a buried note; origin/main did not show the full repeal-and-rewrite sequence as operative text.
current_xml_comparison.detail: The live XML contains only a note buried inside section 1101 and misses the required repeal-marking and the full set of textual rewrites.

final_recommended_action: Apply the express section rewrites to Title 8 sections 1101, 1551, 1102, 1103, 1105, 1182, 1189, 1532, 1533, 1534, and 1481.
Mark the Title 8 repeal in sequence with the reenactments.
Account for the later repeal of section 1102 by PL 19-147.
Remove the Trello link.

laws.title: Public Law 17-128 | Impeachment Procedures and Protocols Act (IPPA)
laws.chronology_conclusion: Operative congressional-procedure enactment with a direct 28 U.S.C. 1365 amendment; no later repeal was identified in the reviewed materials.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Replace 28 U.S.C. 1365 with the supplied congressional subpoena enforcement text.
source_evidence: 28 U.S. Code 1365 shall now read as.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the compliance and rule-of-construction material as congressional-procedure text or a note chain.

laws.title: Public Law 17-129 | Department of Education Act
laws.chronology_conclusion: Operative organic-act style enactment that creates permanent Department of Education text; no later repealer was identified in the reviewed materials.
laws.target_justification: The source is permanent Title 20 organic-act material and should be created as new sections rather than forced into an existing note stub.

source_evidence: The act may be cited as the Department of Education Act.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Codify the definitions, department establishment, principal officers, and Office of Career and Technical Education as new permanent Title 20 sections.
source_evidence: There is established an executive department to be known as the Department of Education.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Codify the transfer and administrative material as operative Title 20 text or a tightly trimmed transfer note chain.
source_evidence: Functions are transferred to the Secretary of Education.
The Secretary is given administrative authority.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the construction, severability, and reference language as savings text attached to the Title 20 enactment.
source_evidence: The act includes severability and reference language.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Keep the effective-date and interim-appointments language as note material.
source_evidence: The bill takes effect immediately after passage and signature.
The source creates a Department of Education.
It includes permanent officers, transfers, and administrative provisions.

current_xml_comparison.branch_vs_origin: The reviewed branch still carries a quoted-content dump; origin/main did not show the organic act properly converted into permanent sections.

final_recommended_action: Codify the organic-act text as real Title 20 sections.
Remove the quoted-content dump and Trello link.
Drop the unsupported repeal or supersession label.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Codify the oath timing and forfeiture rule as a new permanent Title 5 section.

laws.title: Public Law 17-132 | Standard Process for International Extraditions Act
laws.chronology_conclusion: Operative extradition-process statute tied to 18 U.S.C. 3182, but it does not itself amend 3182.
laws.target_justification: The act should sit near the Title 18 extradition provisions, but it does not provide a direct amendment target for 18 U.S.C. 3182.

source_evidence: The act is called the Standard Process for International Extraditions Act.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Create a new definition section describing extradition.
source_evidence: Extradition is defined as the removal of a person for criminal prosecution or punishment.

provisions.enacted_text_or_amendment_command: Preserve the treaty limits, dual-criminality rules, DOJ/State certification, and custody rules as permanent extradition-process text or a closely related note chain.
source_evidence: The act is keyed to 18 U.S. Code 3182.
It sets the extradition process requirements.
The source creates a substantive extradition process.

final_recommended_action: Move the enactment away from Title 50 and into a Title 18-appropriate placement or close statutory note anchor.
